A calf, yes. Pretty little calf. The bark was a big bark and the slope on the backside against the bank there, it sloped way down. Yeah, that's up where those houses are. That used to be a great big bowery there, but they did tour that town. Yeah, they had dances there. Yeah, they had that great big bowery. Yeah. You've seen the picture of the cook wagon, I understand. I had long dresses then. I'm the first one. And then my husband's sister is the other one. I had to be kind of the main boss of what I should say. So I don't see how we could do it. I think they had to have breakfast at five o 'clock.

words: Oh, it was about 25 or 30. They had bundles down there, so they had to go and gather up the bundles. Yeah, they had to have breakfast early, and then I guess dinner at 11. Then they didn't need supper. Then they had lunch in the afternoon. They didn't need supper, so they got through seven, eight o 'clock. And that got late before we got to bed. Oh, I don't understand. Well, you can do most anything while you're young. But I've been thinking many times. We had to bake all the bread in a little bit of a stove. It was room for the tea kettle and the coffee pot. I don't see how in the world we could do it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, would you have to make a big meal, or would they just do it with a lot of one thing?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, we had to have big meals all the time. You had to have a big breakfast, and you had to have a big dinner and a big supper. Meat and potatoes and vegetables and I guess... I had made pudding one day. And when they moved the cook wagon, one pudding, one dish fell out of the oven. And my sister-in-law, she was kind of scared, so she heard and put some wood over it. Well, I didn't care because it fell out, but she was afraid the boss would say something. And I never forgot that, because when this rolled out, she heard and put some sticks of wood over it. But he shouldn't see it. Well, it didn't bother if he said anything. I just said it fell out. So I don't know. I guess we had to make it. Because we never could tell when they were going to... And then we had to pull on to things that it wouldn't fall down, you know. But I guess they shook the stove. So one of those pudding pans fell out. And there's a lot more air than horses on that thing that moved the oven. I don't remember if it was anything left of it, or if it picked up some of it. I don't know.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How often would they move the wagon in a day?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, I don't know. Well, the tall defense of... How big the deal were, I guess. They had to have the cookware in for the clothes where they worked. I don't know. I guess we stayed a few days.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Man, we stayed a few days. A bottle of red chamois. There's a man for every buttery. On that day, there were several men to pick up the box and throw them in the wagon. And that's when they come from the wagon, they had to throw them in the machine. When they come to the machine, there was two spike pitches. They helped all along. Then there was a sub-rated attendant. And then there was two saxors and a jig. That was the... The person took care of the straw pile, so that this wasn't plugged up on. When it was the roustabout. Yeah, he brought the food. Rasped about, then it was the two cooks. Yes, obviously. Yeah, the roustabout is here. Then the boss. Then it was the boss, too. Yeah, there was many.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you eat with the men, or did you have to wait until there was your eating before we went?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, we had to hurry and pour up coffee and all that. We ate after they got through. Yeah. And it wasn't very big wagon, either. It was kind of crowded. They had tables on them benches all along. But they were awful nice. They were awful nice.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: I just cooked one year, right?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: It's hard work, but we had an awful good roustabout. They called him roustabout. He went to town and got all the food. And if he seen something nice to eat, whether we ordered it or not, he brought it home. Once he come with about half a sack of corn, and he sat down and cleaned it, you know, shelled it already for us. So he helped. He was an awful nice man.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was that the roustabout's whole job? Was that the roustabout's whole job?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah, that was his whole job, to go to town and get these. Get these and let them bring them on. And then the gloves and socks and then the things that he did. Well, whatever they wanted. Yeah, his neighbor served her a brewery. He was awful nice here. And we saw him and I'd see him. He helped, you know. He brought home some of the corn and got that all ready so we could put it in the kettle. I don't see how we could find room for the food on that stove. I've been wondering many times. Because they had to have a big coffee pot and a tea kettle. Well, the top of that stove was one of those things. Well, it's one of those small old fashioned stoves. They should have had a big, big stove and they had that many men. I don't see how we could do it. I guess the other wood, they've got a big one in there.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was the rasp about all wood for the stove and everything?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah, he hauled everything. He hauled everything wood for the stove. Water. We slept underneath the cook wagon. And I'd run up first and she said, I don't see how you could run up. My sister and I said, I don't see how you could run up so quick. Well, we were young. We slept good, you know. We jumped up and we had to get breakfast in an hour. I think they had to eat the five o 'clock.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: When somebody come around and wake you up and tell you what it was like?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: No, no, no, no. We woke up. I woke up myself and run up and heard and got breakfast. And then they had lunch. I think they had lunch in the middle of the day. We had to, I guess the rasp, sit for about some sandwiches and coffee. And then bake all the bread. Just think of it. Now they buy everything like that.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How much did you get paid? How much would you get paid for doing that?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, we were supposed to have two dollars a day, but I don't think we got it. I don't think we got paid for the last weeks. I don't remember. It started to rain. Two dollars a day is what we were supposed to have. On the other end of this cook house, there's a 50 gallon wooden barrel that the rasp bought, has to keep full of water for the cooks. Yeah, and you have to have water too. It's just kind of interesting. I never have been so very strong person. It was really too hard almost. Because you didn't get much sleep because you had to hurry if you get to about ten o 'clock with all the dishes and everything. But they were real nice. And the good old days. Yeah, it was just hard work then. They had to pick up the bundles and get them to them when they come over to the machine. They hauled them in the machine and the machine kept on them. They had men that sold. They'd run in sacks and then they sold them. They had to hurry up and sew them quick too, you know. So they wouldn't run over. Of course. Sacks sold. I sold for two dollars a day. Fourteen hours a day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Yeah. Fourteen hours a day.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How many seasons were you a sack filler?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Fourteen.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: How many seasons were you with?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Fourteen seasons. My husband was a superintendent. He looked after the machine. Oh. Yeah, he looked after it. As long as there was nothing wrong with the machine, he didn't have anything to do. But if it was something wrong, he had to repair it. Well, hey, remember boss. Yeah. Yeah, that was in the good old days, I guess. In the good old days, it's different now.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What was it like sewing sacks? How would you have to do it? Huh? How would you sew sacks?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, you had a big needle with a... A big long needle with a twine on it.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: A gunny sack?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: It was about that wide across the top of the spout. It was a regular gunny sack size. Yeah. And you hooked that on a spout. There was a spout that came down and was divided. Yeah, I run down to the side. The empty one. You hit one on this side. When you got yours, you kept jigging it down so you got it pulled. Then you switched it over on the other side. Then the other side, so it had to take care of it over to this side. When you jigged that thing down and take it over here, you had a place to sit down. Then you had to shape it to the top. So you had this side, you drag it in, then you pulled it this side in, and the other over the top.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And you over-lagged it?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Over left. Over left. Then you had your needle hanging right here. Right here. You pulled the string out, and you took your hand like this. And it ended up by that furthest heel. And you went around with this and then this over and caused it uptight. Uh-huh.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: And you started to fix the sack on top. Oh. Well, you fixed the thing. Well, after I fixed it. Right. After you shaped it. And you go, when you get sacked and stuff between your legs, then you shape that ear all furthest away from you. You just kind of pull it together. Then you come over here, and then you get your needle and your thread. And you sit on that, because it's a big skein. And the eye of that needle, you didn't have to sit the end through. You'd have the string in it. You flipped it in on the side. And you pulled that out, and took the string in the left hand, and laid it this way. With one end sticking out. Then you throw this other one out of your right hand, walking around there. Then you took the left hand, and put it over there. And then you pulled that uptight. Oh. Make a loop around the ear. Yeah, a loop around the ear. Make a double loop around the ear. Then you start a sword on the first stitch you took. You took it right underneath the ear. So be sure that this is tight. Then you put 11 stitches in the wheat side. And you got to the other end. Then you put a double loop on that thing and pull it uptight. And then it made an octa. Well, that's all you had to do to thread that thing, was to flip the needle over and give it a pull like that. And it had to tie it in the back end. And it was just lifting. And you had to pick that. Then you reached out here and flipped it back in. Then you had to tip the second carrier. And you had to pick that sack up and put it on your knee and get up. And then walk out that 50-heat resort to make a pile of up.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How heavy were those sacks?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: That had to be back then. I can take care of your sack.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How heavy were those sacks? How heavy?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: About 135 pounds. And then you had to get back and do it all over again. Yeah, just keep on it as fast as they could. Being Clem Harris on that burnt ridge. We went out to Soul Sacks for the burnt ridge we seen for two of us. Paul Sankt, Jacob Houghton was just for two of us. One dollar more in the back of the ridge on the palm crows.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Then we sold the two of us. Sold 800 sacks that day and moved twice. Sold it like the way it is. And we was both soft. We hadn't done hard again in these for a long time. Boy, I tell you there's two that turned me into a man this time. We did arm and arm. Now the worst of it was your hands this time. Uh-huh. Time you sold. That made me, that string, that dang string through it. You get so sore. Oh, boy. Then your hands next to it, it just looked like that. You couldn't go. You couldn't go.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Um, how long were the thrashing season last?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well...

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: One that all depended on the modern grain that the county had to... pretty good. What am I saying, that's from 25 to 30 days, generally. Most of it. But one year, I worked for an old man, Kate. And we dressed down in the Yellow Sea country. We was down there for about 30 days. But when we came back, Johnny was going to go home. We got a bunch of wolves up here by 80 miles. Some stag grain up there. So we moved up there. We put in 10 days up there before we got through. We shook snow off of some of the bottles up there before they got through. They had the last base of the tent. They had those ever-bearing strawberries. My God, we had. We had good, honest-to-God green strawberries for some of the other sugar table. Boy, they were important with the farmers up there. Boy, that, I tell you, that was delicious. Oh, you think I'm good. We could have eaten. You could have eaten the pig if you had it, I guess.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Do you remember what pay you had? Do you remember how much pay you had?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, they went two and a half a day. No, we had two and a four. But then we struck for two bits more. So we had two and a half words to answer.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Do you think the men who owned and operated thrash machines made quite a bit of money?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I don't think they ever got much. Like Charles Smith said, I worked for him one year. He said, I wouldn't have to buy this thing. But if the entire thing is wore out, if I want another thrash, we don't have to go and test again. He said that it should be, you should make enough you could buy another one. But it wore out the necessary factor.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah, it doesn't much pay. We had to work often hard on little pay. Just think the wages people have now. A lot of them have a thousand a month. I know just common laborers have about a thousand a month. Just think of it how people, everything is higher. But then anyway, I wonder how long this big wages will last. I guess nobody knows. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, last time you were telling me about, was it your folks that had a home set above Clarkia? Or was that Ted's?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Oh yeah, no, my dad had a home set above Clarkia. Yeah, but he didn't get anything because it was a railroad script on it. And they didn't tell him that, I guess. So he didn't get anything. He went there many times and had, awful, it was awful hard to get there. They had to have pack horses and way out in the timber. And he built the cabin there and cleared an acre. They were supposed to clear an acre. But then they said a railroad script. He never got anything but the others got about ten thousand for them. The other people that sold them.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did your dad find his homestead right out here where he came?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, I don't know how he found it. I guess there was somebody that told him that it was home sets to take. I guess maybe the people that these smiths out on ..., they maybe told him. Maybe he found that out before he come. I don't know. We never thought about asking anything about that. I guess maybe they told him it was homesteads. My dad couldn't even write. I don't think he hardly went to school. He made an X when he asked on something. But my mother, she could write. She was smart. Well, he wasn't so dumb, but he hardly ever went to school.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You never had the training?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: In Sweden. But she went, so she was. He didn't have time to go to school. Well, I don't know. They said he run around until he was 14, 15 years old. Well, nothing for that mother of yours. No, no, he was. I didn't know anything about their folks or anything.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were the schools like here? What were the schools like here when you went to the schools here? What was it?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: The country schools. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What were they like?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, they were good. We happened to have awful good teachers. We were really kind of a little older, older women. It wasn't kids. Yeah, we went. We didn't get to go, I guess maybe to 5th or 6th grade. That's all we got to go. Because we had to be home and help my mother. We had to be home so much. But we learned real good from we went because I said we had. I was telling you about one teacher. We were so scared, you know, but we had to go up and stand in front. They don't have to do that. We all had to march. And we were so afraid we wouldn't know our lesson. You know, it was just terrible how scared we were. And that teacher, she said, well, it would take 75 angels to be patient with you kids. And I'm not one yet. And I never forgot that. I never forgot what she said. Her name was Miss Aiken. That was up in the country school.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, how would they keep kids in line? Huh? How would they keep the students from getting out of line in those schools? How would someone like Miss Aiken take care of the rowdy boys or something like that?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: It was easy to take care of them. The kids didn't dare to do anything them days. They didn't even dare to turn around. There was a girl that's off a foreign school. And she was, I guess she was trying to copy from me or something. And she just turned her head a little bit, you know, that she didn't dare to turn around. They were off a street because they didn't dare to do anything. I know one boy, he told me after we got going up, I didn't remember.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: He said, don't you remember that you copied from yours and then you slipped it back and gave it to me?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: And their name was Boffman. They had a timber clear way further up than we did. Harry Boffman. No, I said, I don't remember. Oh, yes. He said, you copied and sent it back to me. And then that's the way he went. And I guess it went all right. I guess I'm a little smarter than he was or something. Maybe you paid more attention to the lessons. Well, yeah, the baby wasn't so hard. Some are awful hard. That girl was awful hard to learn. So we went to the... I remember... I don't think I liked the school. The job of being a red.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: The best on the side. We had to walk quite a ways too. You never thought about staying home because there's no or anything like that. It's just to go on to school. I think it's just so deep. It went all right. He took off one side and the other one was... He just got tired. He dropped back and got straight on my eye.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: You were telling me last time about some trouble that in your school one time the teacher wasn't too good?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: No, steel-fed. All that, that was quite a good one. You said he was me. Oh, I remember one time I was in the very back seat of the school. And he held my calipers. He was in the front of me.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: In front of you?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, she hadn't been to school the day before.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: She turned around and we asked whether the job of the lesson was... The old steel-fed, he was down there in the blackboard. But he had a little leopard class. He kept holding the front of him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What did he see then? He turned around and said, yeah, if you like him so why don't you get back there in the second one?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, she just wanted to know about the lesson. When she come over there, I slid over and she slid in. The side of her seat wasn't very big either. It was just me from one person. So when she come back there, the geographers, all of a sudden they're holding them. There's about this square, but when I'm holding them, there's about this wide. Big wide one. So when she come over there, I just set that thing up on end. The big guy in behind that thing. I bet you. I don't think we'd love to have them. We just have to have them talk. Oh, pretty soon I said you better get back to you. That didn't help out then, I think. It's a different thing. On time, we used to go out and play baseball. Well, he always wanted to be out there.

speaker: Rob Moore
Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: He always wanted to be the catcher. Well, I happen to be out first place.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Another locked in. Short foul is Landon right behind the base there. Well, he picked that thing up off there. He had to be in the school. He just hold on. He throw that thing just as hard as he could. And then that guard on baseball, it's stuck. It's stuck like that. Take me back so I just throw it around, throw the right thing. I got them, and we played catch.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Both throw it as hard as he could. He told me not to throw it back and throw it back, throw it back, throw it back, and find I got tired.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I'll throw it way over his head and down into the paper's feet. So I sat down and just sort of took it out. That was the end of it. Well, when he left the school, now he would go to stay after school.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: He made you stay after school? Maybe he gave you a whipping, huh?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, that's what he was thinking.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How old were you then? How old were you?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, I don't know. I was about 15, I guess, somewhere. Maybe 16. Well, I was having to cut back, see. They had one of these great big pocket stoves that stood about that high. They had a long iron stow boat. It had a loop one end and a hook in the other end. It was about 16 long. Well, that hung on the wall right behind him. However, it was just as soon as he turned all the kids out. He started down there, I am. But he got just about where they stowed. So he just got up and stepped up to the seat and got stowed. I said, you and I just about. I had to have all the bags in it. He's a cop. I had to get him. I had to get him just as hard as I could to kill him. But he was just as blue in his face as any man I ever saw.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: But he left me alone. He left you alone. Well, that wasn't anything. I told him them to. I said, if you ever lay a hand on me, I said, if you'd be the last time you lay a hand on me. It would have been. Because if he'd attacked me along the highway on the road, I'd carry a good sharp knife on me. I mean, I might have cut it if I'd had. Oh, my. It wasn't like he was awfully rough. You said he was mean. He laid in the north before he died.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did he mistreat other kids too?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: He put cars from out there. Well, I had them outside of the woods, you know. There was a toy, a lady's toy, on one side and a man's toy. Oh, Arthur, and the children. He went and built her a toilet and it was chock-full. He couldn't hold us no longer. They went in the woods yet. Well, old steel mitts happened to go out there and see that on the wood pile in the wet stub. He didn't know that Arthur Anderson was on the toilet, but he blamed it on the mud cars.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: He told Pudd to stay after school. Pudd knew what was coming. He was open, sweetened that out, he took off and started to run for home. Well, old steel mitts, he took after him. And he couldn't go out. Sorry, Mr. Prush about that moment he started to down the road. He run here from the schoolhouse, cleared out the power of deans where he left them from the other quarter of a mile away. Before he caught him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: And he run after him?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah, caught him and turned him around. He went all the way up to the schoolhouse. He made him across the seat and went in there to test all the delft down top of the seat. That's a kind of a mess.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: It's funny, he went to school anyway afterwards, huh?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I wouldn't have went to school that long. I don't think Pudd ever went back. Well, he caught up in our school too because I don't remember him, but I know he come and hit my sister on the fingers with something. And she didn't like, I guess she thought that was terrible. I guess she never done anything either to mount her anything. So he come and hit her on the fingers with a ruler.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, did he last teaching around here very long? Did he stay in the school around here very long? Did he stay in the school very long? Did he teach very long? How could he have a teacher?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I reckon he was over there one season. I wake up, Carton French. I teach afterwards. Boy, that was a nice man. Yeah, I heard that Steve's interest is bad. Well, I guess he landed. He was kind of a man. But he married a...

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: A child's sister. He was married to a child's sister. Yeah, I didn't know that.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah, I guess he was, I heard he was mean. We happened to have awful good teachers and they were well educated. So we learned good at the time that he went to school.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I suppose you went to college. Oh yeah, I did that. I'm not too sure that you learned that much more in college than you learned in high school back in the old days. Yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did the town of Troy change very much during when prohibition came in?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: No, when prohibition came in. Well, I guess they changed. I guess they fixed, you know, they fixed different streets and... Prohibition. When the saloons went out. Oh yeah.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Were there people around Troy who worked pretty hard to get prohibition?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Will you tell them what it was about? Well, then they got to see if they could do that and handle more saloons here. Well, they all went to the brush and made their own. So it was everybody that had the moonshine at that time.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Was moonshine pretty good?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: They had some awful good moonshine in this country. Some of that brotherhood said, we didn't have to do it. They had some awful good moonshine. You could buy a whole gallon for five dollars.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I think it was dead. There's... I bet it was a lot of that... all this stuff that you just put in. No, no. That's for sure.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, you were telling me last time about how you drew the short straw one time that had to be a bootlegger?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Didn't...

speaker: Rob Moore
words: We were telling you last time about how you drew the short straw or something, had to become a bootlegger?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Well, you had to have a union tab.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did that work, anyway?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, there was Albert... No. I think there was Victor Berg and Adolph Swanson, David Swanson. David Swanson.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: There was one more. Four or five hours. We were in a long capital with just a little waste of money.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: You couldn't get... The only place was Washington and there was a union down in Zulpin. So you just... came before Christmas. Well, they all shipped together. I was sent for a lot of waste. I had a suitcase about that long, about that wide, about that high.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How was it that you got to be the one that went? You were the one that went?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I don't know. We were drawn straws or something.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Who had to go?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I just don't remember how that went. But anyway, I was elected. So I got on the train here. I went to both. I had to transfer there on the branch route, out there. I went over there and bought... I had a return ticket. I bought all the money I had. I had that suitcase back before. I got on the train, come home. It was Christmas Eve. I got in here at... I think the train was about 9 o 'clock. Yeah, it was about 9 o 'clock, I think. 8 .39. I got off down here at the depot.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: He was the cop. He was the cop, yeah. He always met the train. When I got off, I had my suitcase past or in downtown. Well, I got just about behind the hotel now for the alley myself. Well, Terry said you went for it.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Did you what?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: He said you went for it enough. You went for it enough.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: In the hotel?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: In the alley. Oh, in the alley. Well, I didn't have that attitude. I know what you've got in there. He says it's this much longer time for him. I'm going to take it to a funeral. I'll open up this again. I don't know what I said. Go to him.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Couple inches.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I'm high, I said. You're going home now. No, I said I'm going to church. I was doing to the church. New Year's Eve. There was a program up there to the church. It was August Aline, he was in the wilderness. So when I came up, I was supposed to put the linker in August Aline's curtain. I said, London double because the plane. I couldn't find it. It's current or his horses are nothing. I think he must have had a blanket because it was cold. So I just had a suitcase. He says, the vestibule, I've been to the church of an Indian state for a long while. He picked up the old suitcase and then went out and all the rest of the boys that was in there, they were in there so we rode out of the old cab and then the celebrations out there.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Where did you put the suitcase?

words: You found his suitcase. The vestibule. When you went home. The letters, the name of David, all of them, they were in a big spliff. Well, you had the right to come with the moonshine. This is dry country. I know you were proud to bring the whiskey in you. I was against the law. I was breaking the law. I was like, oh, that's it. You don't want to push that. That would have been a lot of it. And that would have been that haze when he'd have taken the whole thing from you. More than that, I didn't see him. He didn't push. Well, you're lucky he wasn't. words: He was nice. He was nice. He was the one. He just wanted a drink. He didn't care. There were a lot of that rasping. He wasn't there when they were. He's never bothered nobody. Unless there was a rally or something that he could be bad to do something. No, it wasn't. He was not. He was not.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: After the saloon closed down, where did people go to have their parties or to get together to kind of meet each other?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Because you couldn't do it in the saloon anymore.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: There was no saloon there. Yeah, I don't know what they did. Really. We were so young then that I don't remember what happened. Well, they had a lot of parties out in the country. Yeah, and they had a lot of parties out to different people. Going across to the other. Yeah, they had a lot of parties then in their homes. And there's a lot of doings in the churches too that they don't care about. Yeah, there's a vacant house up there called a old chamfer place. This was a pretty good sized house. Kitchen and a couple of bedrooms. Then they had a big front room. It was about the same with the sis. But it was long enough that it even took you out there to the porch maybe more. They used to go there, get somebody to play the violin or something. Everybody would go over there and when they kept the place clean, they'd go over there and have party meetings and dance.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: This is when you were a young man?

speaker: Rob Moore
words: Yeah, this is the good old place.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah, you were sure. And all that, they used to have a lot of fun.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: How did courtship go in those days? How would you go to ... or things like that?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, I guess they just went to the house and took off the girl.

speaker: Rob Moore
words: What kind of things would you do?

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Well, you generally go to a house someplace where they had a party. And if it was celebration, some celebration, sometimes they had to mosque or you'd go there. You'd go wherever there was a celebration and sometimes to church too. Then you'd meet somebody and you'd get to talk. The first thing is all the other people around there. So they'd go and study and all of a sudden they hear that it's married. The way it went.

words: Well, I think they went with different ones. There might be some that married the one that first went with. Some of them did, I guess. But they were just something like that. They'd go with different fellows.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: Yeah.

words: Well, yeah, I think maybe they were. People were more friendly, I think. Because they were together. They didn't have any ways. They all had to go with horses and above your sled or something. Well, it was probably a bit too slow to get out of the way they thought. They kind of split up the younger generation from having their get-togethers like they used to. They kind of just gradually danced away, the parties and stuff like that. Those were the things that we thought.

They all went, you know, if it was a dance in the school last day, they all went there in a hall someplace.

speaker: Ida Swanberg Asplund or Theodore Sundell
words: I don't know. I don't know. I guess they were more happy. Young people, you know, they always have other comments. But there's a whole bunch up there that go to church. The grandmas, all them young folks up there. Well, they walked over and they walked home. Well, good God, that whole bunch will go together and go home and all that other thing and have a good time, all the way from the type of left church that they got home. Yeah, they were... I said young. It's different when you were young than when you were older.
